Front: When should you use OpenStreetMap data instead of Google or Apple map data?
Back: Use OSM when you need open reuse, redistribution, offline access, editability, or crisis mapping; commercial maps are optimized for proprietary consumer navigation and often restrict derived datasets.

Front: What is the main operational risk of using OpenStreetMap as a production basemap tile source?
Back: OSM data is open, but the public tile servers are not a free production CDN; heavy apps should self-host, cache properly, or use a tile provider.

Front: When is Overture Maps a better starting point than raw OSM extracts?
Back: Use Overture when you want normalized, release-versioned, cloud-queryable base map themes such as places, buildings, roads, boundaries, and base features in GeoParquet.

Front: What problem does Overture Maps solve that OSM intentionally does not fully solve?
Back: Overture reconciles multiple sources into consistent schemas and releases; OSM prioritizes open volunteer editing, which creates variable coverage and tagging inconsistency.

Front: Why is a basemap different from your operational data layer?
Back: A basemap provides geographic context such as roads, labels, terrain, and water; your operational layer carries the task-specific data being analyzed or interacted with.

Front: When should you choose a vector basemap instead of a raster basemap?
Back: Choose vector when you need dynamic styling, language changes, layer filtering, feature interaction, or smooth high-DPI rendering; raster is simpler but fixed.

Front: What problem does the slippy map pattern solve?
Back: It lets a browser pan and zoom across large maps by requesting only the tiles currently needed, reusing cached tiles and avoiding full-world downloads.

Front: Why does a tile pyramid matter for performance?
Back: It precomputes data at multiple zoom levels so the client or server reads an appropriate resolution instead of resampling full-detail data for every view.

Front: What is the key difference between raster tiles and vector tiles?
Back: Raster tiles are pre-rendered images; vector tiles contain clipped geometry and attributes that the client styles and renders.

Front: When is GeoJSON the wrong response format for a web map?
Back: When the dataset is large or pan/zoom-driven; use vector tiles so the browser receives only viewport-relevant features at appropriate detail.

Front: What does an MVT tile contain?
Back: An MVT tile contains one or more layers of clipped, simplified vector features encoded as compact Protobuf geometry and properties in tile-local coordinates.

Front: Why are vector tile coordinates usually local integers instead of global lon/lat floats?
Back: Local integer coordinates make tiles compact and fast to render; the renderer maps them back to the tile bounds and screen position.

Front: Why are vector tiles lossy for analysis?
Back: They are clipped, simplified, and zoom-dependent for visualization, so they may omit or alter geometry needed for exact counts, joins, or measurements.

Front: When is PMTiles a good fit?
Back: Use PMTiles for static or periodically regenerated tile pyramids that should be served directly from object storage or a CDN without a tile backend.

Front: When is PMTiles a poor fit compared with GeoParquet?
Back: PMTiles is for tiled visualization; GeoParquet is better when you need original vector features for analytics, joins, filtering, and exact geometry.

Front: What does PMTiles replace in a simple web-map architecture?
Back: It can replace millions of individual tile files or a live tile server with one range-readable archive served from static storage.

Front: Why does PMTiles use spatial ordering such as a Hilbert curve?
Back: Spatially nearby tiles are stored near each other, so viewport requests tend to require fewer and more coherent byte-range reads.

Front: When should you use MapLibre GL JS?
Back: Use MapLibre GL JS when you need an open-source browser renderer for interactive vector-tile maps with WebGL styling, camera controls, and layer interaction.

Front: Why did MapLibre become important after Mapbox GL JS v2?
Back: MapLibre preserved the open-source web map renderer ecosystem after Mapbox moved GL JS v2 to a more restrictive proprietary license.

Front: What is the practical difference between MapLibre and deck.gl?
Back: MapLibre renders the base map and style layers; deck.gl specializes in high-performance analytical overlays such as millions of points, arcs, grids, and 3D layers.

Front: When should deck.gl be layered over MapLibre?
Back: Use MapLibre for geographic context and deck.gl when your main problem is rendering large or custom analytical overlays efficiently.

Front: What does Protomaps bundle together?
Back: Protomaps combines OSM-derived basemap data, PMTiles archives, and tooling so maps can be self-hosted from static storage.

Front: Why is OpenMapTiles useful even if you do not use its hosted service?
Back: It defines a repeatable OSM-to-vector-tile schema and toolchain, making self-hosted basemaps more interoperable with common styles and renderers.

Front: When should you use a live tile server instead of PMTiles?
Back: Use a live tile server when tiles must reflect frequently changing database state, query-time filtering, user-specific styling, or dynamic aggregation.

Front: When is a static PMTiles deployment simpler than pg_tileserv or Martin?
Back: Use PMTiles when the tileset is mostly static, traffic may be high, and you want CDN/object-storage hosting without operating a database-backed tile service.

Front: What is the difference between DEM, DSM, and DTM?
Back: DEM is the generic elevation raster; DSM is top-surface elevation including trees and buildings; DTM is bare-earth terrain with above-ground objects removed.
Source: Digital Elevation Model.md; Digital Surface Model.md; Digital Terrain Model.md

Front: What does a canopy height model calculate?
Back: A CHM is typically DSM minus DTM, estimating vegetation height above bare ground at each pixel.

Front: Why does raw LiDAR or photogrammetry usually produce a DSM before a DTM?
Back: The first reconstruction sees visible surfaces such as roofs, trees, and ground; producing a DTM requires classifying and filtering to ground points.
Source: Digital Terrain Model.md; Digital Surface Model.md

Front: When is LiDAR better than photogrammetry for terrain under trees?
Back: LiDAR can produce multiple returns and often reaches gaps in canopy to estimate ground; passive imagery generally sees the top visible surface.

Front: What makes point density a planning variable in LiDAR acquisition?
Back: Point density controls what can be extracted: sparse data may support DEMs, while dense data supports buildings, individual trees, powerlines, and inspection features.

Front: Why does airborne LiDAR need GPS plus IMU?
Back: The range measurement only gives distance from the sensor; GPS and IMU provide the sensor position and orientation needed to place each return in world coordinates.

Front: When should COPC be preferred over plain LAZ?
Back: Use COPC when a LAZ point cloud needs cloud-native spatial subsetting through an internal octree and HTTP range requests.
Source: Cloud-Optimized Point Cloud.md; Light Detection and Ranging.md

Front: What is the main difference between LiDAR and SAR as active sensors?
Back: LiDAR uses laser pulses and produces point clouds or elevations; SAR uses microwave radar backscatter and produces coherent imagery sensitive to structure, moisture, and phase.
Source: Light Detection and Ranging.md; Synthetic Aperture Radar.md

Front: When is SAR the right sensing choice despite not looking like a photo?
Back: Use SAR when clouds, darkness, moisture, roughness, deformation, or metallic structure matter more than natural-color visual interpretation.

Front: Why can SAR image through clouds and at night?
Back: SAR actively transmits microwave pulses and measures returned backscatter, so it does not depend on sunlight and is less blocked by cloud cover than optical imagery.

Front: What does SAR phase enable that amplitude-only imagery cannot?
Back: Phase enables interferometry and subtle change or deformation analysis; amplitude-only products throw away much of the coherent signal.

Front: What is the tradeoff between SAR spotlight and ScanSAR modes?
Back: Spotlight dwells on a small area for high resolution; ScanSAR covers a wider swath with coarser resolution.

Front: How do SAR bands change what you can infer?
Back: Shorter wavelengths emphasize surface detail and texture; longer wavelengths penetrate deeper into vegetation, soil, or ice and support biomass or subsurface-related analysis.

Front: What does SAR polarization help distinguish?
Back: Co-pol channels often emphasize surface or double-bounce scattering; cross-pol channels often highlight volume scattering such as vegetation canopy.

Front: When does InSAR apply?
Back: Use InSAR when phase differences between SAR acquisitions can estimate elevation or surface deformation, such as subsidence, earthquakes, volcanoes, or ice motion.
Source: Interferometric Synthetic Aperture Radar.md; Synthetic Aperture Radar.md

Front: What does DInSAR add to basic InSAR?
Back: DInSAR compares interferograms or removes topographic phase to isolate surface change or deformation over time.

Front: Why does coregistration matter in change detection?
Back: Images must line up pixel-to-pixel; otherwise apparent change may be misregistration rather than real surface change.

Front: What is the difference between mosaicking and compositing?
Back: Mosaicking stitches adjacent or overlapping scenes into one spatial product; compositing selects or combines observations over time or criteria such as cloud-free pixels.

Front: Why orthorectify an aerial or satellite image before measurement?
Back: Orthorectification removes terrain, sensor angle, and camera distortion so the image can function as map-accurate geometry.

Front: When are GCPs still useful if an image has RPCs and a DEM?
Back: GCPs improve absolute accuracy by tying the model to surveyed ground coordinates, especially for precision mapping or cross-sensor change detection.

Front: What does photogrammetry solve?
Back: Photogrammetry extracts 3D geometry from overlapping photographs by matching features, estimating camera poses, and triangulating points.

Front: How do SfM and MVS relate in a modern photogrammetry pipeline?
Back: SfM estimates camera poses and sparse structure; MVS densifies the reconstruction into dense point clouds, meshes, DEMs, or orthomosaics.

Front: Why does drone photogrammetry need overlap and varied viewpoints?
Back: Overlapping views provide feature matches and parallax; without enough overlap and geometry, camera pose and 3D reconstruction become weak or fail.

Front: What is the core difference between remote sensing and Earth observation?
Back: Remote sensing is measurement without physical contact; Earth observation is information gathering about Earth, often using remote sensing but not limited to it.

Front: When should active remote sensing be preferred over passive optical sensing?
Back: Use active sensing when you need your own illumination, range measurement, cloud/night capability, or structural backscatter rather than reflected sunlight.

Front: Why does spatial resolution not fully describe a remote sensing product?
Back: You also need spectral, radiometric, and temporal resolution to know what wavelengths, intensity levels, and revisit cadence the product supports.

Front: When is Level 2 remote sensing data often more usable than Level 1?
Back: Level 2 contains derived geophysical variables or corrected products closer to analysis needs; Level 1 is closer to calibrated sensor observations.

Front: Why must remote-sensing classification products be validated?
Back: Without independent reference data and accuracy assessment, map outputs may look plausible while hiding class imbalance, seasonal bias, or poor generalization.

Front: What is a GIS in practical workflow terms?
Back: A GIS stores, analyzes, joins, transforms, and visualizes spatial data so location can be used as a query and analysis dimension.

Front: What does Tobler's First Law warn you to expect in spatial data?
Back: Nearby things tend to be more related than distant things, so spatial autocorrelation can break assumptions behind random sampling and train/test splits.

Front: Why is geodesy not the same as cartography?
Back: Geodesy measures and models Earth shape, position, and reference frames; cartography designs maps for communication.

Front: What is the practical difference between geolocation and geocoding?
Back: Geolocation estimates where something is; geocoding turns an address or place description into coordinates, while reverse geocoding turns coordinates into a place description.

Front: When is geotagging not enough to georeference data?
Back: Geotagging attaches a location to an item; georeferencing ties pixels, geometry, or coordinates into a spatial reference system so measurements and overlays work.

Front: What failure mode does geofencing inherit from geolocation?
Back: If the position estimate, CRS, datum, or boundary geometry is wrong, the geofence decision can be wrong even if the rule itself is configured correctly.

Front: Why is coordinate order a common geospatial bug?
Back: GeoJSON and most web APIs use longitude, latitude, while humans often say latitude, longitude; swapped values can place data in the wrong ocean or hemisphere.

Front: When should EPSG:4326 be used?
Back: Use EPSG:4326 for WGS84 geographic lon/lat storage, GPS-like coordinates, GeoJSON, and API interchange, not for planar distance or area calculations.

Front: When should EPSG:3857 be used?
Back: Use Web Mercator EPSG:3857 for web map tile display, not for accurate area or distance analysis because distortion grows at high latitudes.

Front: Why are distance calculations in raw EPSG:4326 dangerous?
Back: Coordinates are degrees, not meters; planar Euclidean distance on lon/lat produces meaningless or location-dependent results.

Front: When should you project to a local CRS instead of using Web Mercator?
Back: Use a local projected CRS for accurate measurement, buffering, and area calculations in a limited region.

Front: What is an EPSG code useful for?
Back: An EPSG code is a compact identifier for a CRS or coordinate operation, letting software avoid ambiguous projection and datum descriptions.

Front: How does SRID relate to EPSG in databases?
Back: An SRID is the spatial reference identifier stored with geometries; it often matches an EPSG code, but the important point is that the database knows the CRS.

Front: When is UTM a good projection choice?
Back: Use UTM for local or regional work within one zone when you need meter units and lower distortion than global web projections.

Front: Why does MGRS exist if lat/lon already works?
Back: MGRS provides a compact grid reference useful for field, military, and map-sheet workflows where grid squares and precision levels are easier to communicate than decimal degrees.

Front: What is the antimeridian failure mode?
Back: Geometries crossing 180 degrees longitude can be interpreted as spanning the long way around the world unless split or wrapped correctly.

Front: How should GeoJSON crossing the antimeridian usually be handled?
Back: Split the geometry at the antimeridian so coordinates remain unambiguous in the -180 to 180 longitude range.

Front: Why does datum choice matter even when coordinates look plausible?
Back: A datum defines the reference model for coordinates; the same numeric coordinate in different datums can refer to different real-world locations.

Front: What does PROJ do in the geospatial stack?
Back: PROJ performs CRS transformations and geodetic operations, including projections, conversions, and datum transformations.

Front: What problem does GDAL solve?
Back: GDAL provides a common raster and vector data model plus drivers and command-line tools for reading, writing, converting, and processing many geospatial formats.

Front: How do GEOS, PROJ, and GDAL differ?
Back: GEOS handles geometry predicates and operations; PROJ handles CRS transformations; GDAL handles geospatial file formats and raster/vector I/O.
Source: GEOS.md; PROJ.md; Geospatial Data Abstraction Library.md

Front: When should you use QGIS instead of writing code?
Back: Use QGIS for visual inspection, quick editing, cartographic layout, CRS sanity checks, and exploratory workflows where interactive feedback matters.

Front: When is ArcGIS mainly attractive despite open-source alternatives?
Back: ArcGIS is attractive for enterprise GIS governance, Esri ecosystem integration, managed services, licensing support, and organizations already standardized on Esri workflows.

Front: What is the practical difference between vector and raster data?
Back: Vector represents discrete features as geometries with attributes; raster represents continuous fields or imagery as grids of pixel values.

Front: When should vector be chosen over raster?
Back: Use vector for discrete objects with meaningful boundaries, attributes, and topology such as parcels, roads, incidents, and administrative areas.

Front: When should raster be chosen over vector?
Back: Use raster for continuous surfaces, imagery, model grids, elevation, temperature, land cover, and other cell-based measurements.

Front: Why is point cloud a separate data model rather than just vector points?
Back: Point clouds contain massive 3D samples with attributes such as intensity, return number, scan angle, and classification; they need specialized storage and indexing.

Front: When is GeoJSON the right format?
Back: Use GeoJSON for small web API responses, configuration boundaries, examples, and client-side map layers where human-readable JSON and broad support matter.

Front: Why is Shapefile still common but usually not ideal?
Back: It is universally supported legacy GIS exchange, but has split files, 2GB limits, short field names, weak types, and encoding/projection gotchas.

Front: When is GeoPackage a good replacement for Shapefile?
Back: Use GeoPackage for local exchange or storage when you want a single SQLite-based file with multiple layers, better attribute types, CRS support, and spatial indexes.

Front: When is FlatGeobuf a strong choice?
Back: Use FlatGeobuf for large static vector datasets that need fast streaming or spatial bbox queries directly from object storage or a CDN.

Front: What is FlatGeobuf bad at?
Back: It lacks efficient non-spatial attribute indexing and in-place edits, so it is poor for transactional editing or filtering primarily by attributes.

Front: Why does full-file gzip compression break FlatGeobuf's main advantage?
Back: It prevents byte-range random access to indexed features, forcing the reader to decompress sequentially instead of fetching only relevant feature bytes.

Front: When is GeoParquet the right vector format?
Back: Use GeoParquet for analytics-first vector data lakes where column pruning, row-group filtering, cloud scans, and SQL engines like DuckDB or Spark matter.

Front: Why is GeoParquet not a complete web-map serving solution?
Back: It preserves analytical features but lacks visualization overviews or viewport tiling, so browsers may still need too much data at low zooms.

Front: What does GeoArrow add to GeoParquet workflows?
Back: GeoArrow defines efficient in-memory columnar geometry layouts, reducing serialization cost after GeoParquet data is loaded for computation.

Front: When should COG be preferred over classic GeoTIFF?
Back: Use COG when raster imagery or DEMs must be read remotely by spatial window or overview without downloading the whole file.

Front: What two internal features make a COG cloud-optimized?
Back: Internal tiling and overview pyramids let readers fetch the right resolution and spatial chunks using HTTP range requests.

Front: Why is every COG a GeoTIFF but not every GeoTIFF a COG?
Back: COG is a GeoTIFF with a specific internal layout for range-readable cloud access; ordinary GeoTIFFs may lack tiling, overviews, or header organization.

Front: When should Zarr be preferred over COG?
Back: Use Zarr for large multidimensional arrays such as climate, weather, ocean, or time-series data cubes with chunked access across space, time, variables, or levels.

Front: What is the main operational downside of Zarr on object storage?
Back: A large Zarr store can contain many objects, creating request, listing, movement, and lifecycle-management overhead.

Front: Why is STAC commonly paired with COGs?
Back: STAC tells clients what assets exist, where they are, and their metadata; COG lets clients read the raster assets efficiently by spatial window.

Front: What is the atomic unit a STAC user usually cares about?
Back: A STAC Item, which is a GeoJSON feature describing one scene, granule, or observation and linking to one or more assets.

Front: How do STAC Catalog, Collection, Item, and Asset differ?
Back: Catalog links things together; Collection groups related items with extents and shared metadata; Item describes one observation; Assets are the actual files.

Front: When is STAC the wrong abstraction?
Back: If the geometry is the data itself, such as building footprints or parcels, use feature APIs or vector formats; STAC is for metadata about assets.

Front: When is a dynamic STAC API better than static STAC files?
Back: Use an API for search by bbox, datetime, collection, and attributes; static catalogs are simpler but poor for large-scale search.

Front: When is a static STAC catalog attractive?
Back: Use static STAC when metadata can live as files on object storage, updates are simple, and crawling or bulk access matters more than rich search.

Front: What is the main STAC interoperability gotcha?
Back: STAC is flexible, so different providers may use extensions and fields differently; users must inspect item assets, extensions, and provider conventions.

Front: What problem does cloud-native geospatial solve?
Back: It lets clients discover, subset, and process massive geospatial data in place on object storage rather than downloading whole files first.

Front: What is the shared design pattern behind COG, COPC, FlatGeobuf, PMTiles, and GeoParquet?
Back: Metadata or indexes point to spatially or logically relevant chunks, allowing partial reads through range requests or object-level chunk access.

Front: Why is object storage central to cloud-native geospatial?
Back: Object storage provides cheap durable storage and HTTP access, while compute can be ephemeral, distributed, and moved to the data.

Front: When should you avoid forcing a dataset into a cloud-native format?
Back: Avoid it when access patterns are small, local, rarely repeated, or transactional enough that conversion and operational complexity outweigh partial-read benefits.

Front: What is the difference between Spatial SQL and desktop GIS analysis?
Back: Spatial SQL expresses geometry operations as database queries, making joins, filters, aggregations, and indexes part of repeatable data workflows.

Front: When should PostGIS be used instead of DuckDB Spatial?
Back: Use PostGIS for multi-user services, transactional updates, persistent databases, concurrent clients, and production spatial APIs.

Front: When should DuckDB Spatial be used instead of PostGIS?
Back: Use DuckDB Spatial for single-process analytics, notebooks, local ETL, scanning Parquet or GeoParquet, and fast medium-scale exploratory work without a server.

Front: Why does PostGIS have both geometry and geography types?
Back: Geometry is fast planar math in a CRS; geography treats lon/lat on Earth for meter-based distance and area, trading speed for global correctness.

Front: What is the safe PostGIS rule for distance in lon/lat data?
Back: Store `geometry(Point, 4326)` for compatibility and indexing, then cast to `geography` or project locally when accurate meters are needed.

Front: What failure mode does a missing spatial index create in PostGIS?
Back: Spatial predicates may become full table scans, forcing exact geometry checks against every row instead of indexed candidate filtering.

Front: What does OGC Simple Features standardize?
Back: It standardizes common 2D geometry types, WKT/WKB encodings, and spatial predicates/functions used by GIS tools and databases.

Front: When should OGC API Features be used instead of STAC?
Back: Use OGC API Features to serve actual feature collections such as parcels, roads, or sensors; use STAC to catalog assets such as imagery files.

Front: What is OGC API Processes for in a geospatial architecture?
Back: It provides a standard way to expose geospatial processing jobs through web APIs, useful when workflows need server-side execution rather than file discovery.

Front: What problem does a spatial index solve that a B-tree cannot?
Back: A B-tree orders one-dimensional values; spatial indexes organize two- or three-dimensional proximity and bounding relationships for efficient spatial queries.

Front: Why does an index on latitude plus an index on longitude not solve proximity search well?
Back: Each index is one-dimensional, so the database still scans large strips or rectangles and filters many candidates outside the true search radius.

Front: What is the two-phase pattern in spatial-indexed queries?
Back: The index first returns approximate candidates by bounding boxes or cells; exact geometry predicates then filter false positives.

Front: Why do R-trees use bounding rectangles?
Back: Bounding rectangles cheaply approximate complex geometry so whole branches can be skipped when they cannot intersect a query window.

Front: When is GiST the safe default in PostGIS?
Back: Use GiST for most geometry columns and mixed shape queries because it supports PostGIS R-tree-like indexing over points, lines, and polygons.

Front: When might SP-GiST be better than GiST?
Back: SP-GiST can be better for certain clustered point data because it partitions space into non-overlapping regions, but it is less general for complex polygons.

Front: When is a QuadTree conceptually useful?
Back: Use QuadTree thinking for recursively subdividing 2D space into four regions, especially for tiling, simple spatial partitioning, or roughly uniform point data.

Front: What is an Octree used for?
Back: An Octree extends QuadTree subdivision to 3D space and is useful for point clouds, 3D tiles, and volumetric spatial indexing.

Front: Why are space-filling curves useful in geospatial storage?
Back: They map multidimensional space to one-dimensional order while preserving locality enough to cluster nearby features or tiles on disk.

Front: Why is the Hilbert curve often preferred over simpler ordering?
Back: Hilbert ordering tends to preserve spatial locality better than many simpler curves, improving chunk clustering and range-read efficiency.

Front: When should H3 be used?
Back: Use H3 for hierarchical hexagonal aggregation, neighborhood smoothing, grid-based visualization, and approximate spatial joins over point-like events.

Front: Why is H3 not a replacement for PostGIS geometry indexes?
Back: H3 cells are approximations and require coverings or second-pass filters for polygons; GiST geometry indexes support exact predicates over actual shapes.

Front: What is the main H3 resolution gotcha?
Back: Too coarse loses signal; too fine creates sparse cells and slower aggregation, sometimes implying false precision beyond the input data accuracy.

Front: Why do H3 pentagons matter?
Back: H3 has 12 pentagonal cells from its icosahedral construction, which can behave differently in neighbor algorithms and create subtle edge cases.

Front: How does H3 differ from Geohash?
Back: H3 uses hierarchical hexagon-like cells with consistent neighbor relationships; Geohash uses rectangular string prefixes that are simple but distort and have neighbor edge cases.

Front: When is Geohash still attractive?
Back: Use Geohash when you need a simple string prefix index that works in ordinary databases without geospatial extensions.

Front: What does S2 provide compared with H3?
Back: S2 partitions the sphere into hierarchical cells derived from cube faces and is useful for spatial covering, indexing, and region approximation on the sphere.

Front: What is a DGGS in practical terms?
Back: A DGGS references Earth using structured cell identifiers instead of only coordinates, enabling multiresolution grids for indexing, aggregation, and analysis.

Front: When should you be skeptical of global cell-grid analysis?
Back: Grid choice, resolution, cell area variation, boundary effects, and false precision can change analytical conclusions.

Front: What is GeoPandas best for?
Back: GeoPandas is best for Pythonic vector analysis on GeoDataFrames: reading files, spatial joins, buffers, overlays, and exploratory geospatial workflows.

Front: What does Shapely provide under GeoPandas?
Back: Shapely provides geometry objects and operations such as intersection, containment, buffering, and validity checks.

Front: When should you reach for PyProj?
Back: Use PyProj when code needs CRS transformations, projection definitions, or datum-aware coordinate operations through PROJ.

Front: What does Fiona traditionally handle in Python geospatial workflows?
Back: Fiona handles vector file I/O through GDAL/OGR, though newer stacks increasingly use pyogrio for faster GeoPandas reads and writes.

Front: When should rasterio be used instead of GeoPandas?
Back: Use rasterio for raster datasets such as GeoTIFFs and COGs, pixel windows, bands, transforms, and raster reprojection; GeoPandas is for vector features.

Front: What is GeoAlchemy2 for?
Back: GeoAlchemy2 adds PostGIS geometry/geography types and spatial functions to SQLAlchemy workflows, but geometry serialization and migrations still need care.

Front: When is Apache Sedona appropriate?
Back: Use Sedona when spatial joins and geometry operations need to run across distributed Spark-scale datasets rather than single-machine or database workflows.

Front: When is Google Earth Engine the right tool?
Back: Use GEE for planetary-scale remote-sensing analysis with hosted datasets and server-side computation, accepting platform constraints and less open deployability.

Front: Why did cloud-native geospatial emerge partly as an answer to GEE?
Back: It recreates discovery, access, and scalable analysis with open standards and object storage instead of relying on one proprietary hosted platform.

Front: When should Cesium be chosen over MapLibre?
Back: Use Cesium for globe, terrain, 3D tiles, massive 3D city models, point clouds, and time-dynamic 3D scenes rather than flat 2D web maps.

Front: What does a geotransform define for a raster?
Back: It maps pixel coordinates to real-world coordinates using origin, pixel size, rotation, and sign conventions, together with the raster CRS.

Front: Why is a raster geotransform not enough without a CRS?
Back: The transform maps pixels into coordinate numbers, but the CRS defines what those numbers mean on Earth.

Front: When is kepler.gl useful?
Back: Use kepler.gl for quick exploratory visualization of large geospatial datasets, especially point, trip, arc, grid, and H3-style layers.

Front: When is leafmap useful?
Back: Use leafmap for notebook-based exploratory geospatial analysis, quick maps, raster inspection, and teaching rather than production web apps.

Front: When is geemap the right choice?
Back: Use geemap when you want to work with Google Earth Engine from Python notebooks instead of the Earth Engine JavaScript interface.

Front: When is Lonboard useful?
Back: Lonboard is useful for fast notebook visualization of large GeoArrow or GeoParquet-backed data through deck.gl.

Front: What problem does TorchGeo solve for ML pipelines?
Back: TorchGeo handles geospatial-specific data loading, CRS alignment, sampling, multispectral bands, and dataset splits that ordinary torchvision pipelines ignore.

Front: Why can random train/test splits be dangerous in geospatial ML?
Back: Spatial autocorrelation lets nearby samples leak information across splits, producing overly optimistic accuracy estimates.
Source: TorchGeo.md; Tobler's First Law of Geography.md

Front: When should TorchGeo be preferred over torchvision?
Back: Use TorchGeo for satellite, aerial, multispectral, geospatial labels, patch sampling, and CRS-aware datasets; use torchvision for ordinary natural-image workflows.

Front: What is Element84 Earth Search best used for?
Back: Use Earth Search as a STAC API to find cloud-hosted Sentinel, Landsat, NAIP, and related assets on AWS by bbox, time, collection, and metadata filters.

Front: Why is Earth on AWS not itself a data access API?
Back: It is a broad AWS geospatial landing page and program umbrella; actual dataset discovery often happens through registries or STAC APIs like Earth Search.

Front: When should you use NASA Earthdata?
Back: Use NASA Earthdata as the primary portal and access ecosystem for NASA Earth observation datasets and documentation.

Front: When should you use USGS EarthExplorer?
Back: Use EarthExplorer for USGS data discovery and downloads such as Landsat, aerial imagery, elevation, and other Earth science products.

Front: When is OpenTopography a good first stop?
Back: Use OpenTopography to discover and access high-resolution topography, LiDAR, DEMs, and on-demand terrain products for a study area.

Front: Why is USGS 3DEP important?
Back: 3DEP provides broad, free, high-quality U.S. elevation and LiDAR coverage that would be extremely expensive to acquire privately.

Front: What does a contour line represent?
Back: A contour connects locations of equal elevation, making terrain shape readable on a 2D map.

Front: When is hillshade useful?
Back: Hillshade is useful for visualizing terrain relief by simulating illumination, but it is a visualization product, not a measurement of slope or elevation itself.

Front: How do slope and aspect differ?
Back: Slope measures terrain steepness; aspect measures the compass direction a slope faces.

Front: When is a watershed concept applied?
Back: Use watershed analysis when flow direction, drainage basins, catchments, runoff, or upstream/downstream relationships matter.

Front: What does Terrain Ruggedness Index try to capture?
Back: TRI summarizes local elevation variability to quantify how rough or rugged terrain is around each cell.

Front: What is the practical difference between map and imagery?
Back: A map is a symbolic, styled representation of selected geographic information; imagery is sensor-observed pixels that may need interpretation and correction.

Front: Why does cartography matter even when the data is correct?
Back: Bad symbolization, projection, classification, color, or labeling can make correct data misleading or unusable.
